coredata.dc:title: Positive curvature in Stern-Volmer plot described by a generalized model for static quenching
coredata.dc:creator: Ciotta, E.
Prosposito, P.
Pizzoferrato, R.
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescence quenching effect of unfolded-fullerene nanoparticles (UFNPs) in the presence of Cu2+ ions in water solution has been investigated for a broad range of fluorophore/quencher concentrations. The experimental Stern-Volmer plot exhibited a nonlinear behaviour with an initial upward curvature even though carbon nanoparticles are known to form stable complexes with metal ions and thus a dominant static quenching with a linear plot is expected according to the classical Stern-Volmer equation. A generalized model for static quenching, applicable to any value of fluorophore/quencher concentrations, provided a very good fit with the experimental data without the need to introduce any second-order effect, such as in the sphere of action model or diffusion-limited processes. Moreover, by changing the dilution of the UFNP solution, we could study how the quenching ratio depended on the fluorophore concentration, whereas it is predicted to be constant by the standard model. In addition to providing a significant test of the nonlinear model for static quenching, we believe this study could help find the best parameters for the optimization of sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio in applications such as fluorescence sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence quenching
Stern-Volmer equation
Nonlinear quenching

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the University of Rome Tor Vergata in the framework of “GHOST - GrapHene Oxide quantum dots in Sol-gel maTerials for environmental sensing applications” project, whithin Mission: Sustainability" program (D.R. 2817/2016), grant number (CUP): E86C18000450005 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Possible structure of UNFPs and (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of UNFP water suspension.

Fluorescence quenching of UNFPs in the presence of Cu2+ ions in water solution where fluorescence intensity measurements are taken after each incremental addition of Cu2+ up to the maximum molarity of 175 μM.

Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching of UNFPs in the presence of Cu2+ ions in two different water solutions at a fluorophore estimated concentration of [UFNP]1 = 30 μM (solid circles) and [UFNP]2 = 60 μM (triangles). The solid curves represent the plot of Eq. (3).

Fractional fluorescence quenching of UNFPs in the presence of Cu2+ at a concentration of [Cu2+] = 50 μM as a function of the dilution factor of the UFNP solution. The fluorophore estimated concentration [UFNP] is also reported on the top axis. The solid curve represents the model according to Eq. (4).

head.ce:title: Positive curvature in Stern-Volmer plot described by a generalized model for static quenching

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Engineering University of Rome Tor Vergata, 00133 Rome, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Industrial Engineering University of Rome Tor Vergata

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: INSTM and CiMER University of Rome Tor Vergata

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Dipartimento di Ingegneria Industriale, Università di Roma Tor Vergata, Via del Politecnico 1, 00133 Rome, Italy.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dipartimento di Ingegneria Industriale, Università di Roma Tor Vergata

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence quenching effect of unfolded-fullerene nanoparticles (UFNPs) in the presence of Cu2+ ions in water solution has been investigated for a broad range of fluorophore/quencher concentrations. The experimental Stern-Volmer plot exhibited a nonlinear behaviour with an initial upward curvature even though carbon nanoparticles are known to form stable complexes with metal ions and thus a dominant static quenching with a linear plot is expected according to the classical Stern-Volmer equation. A generalized model for static quenching, applicable to any value of fluorophore/quencher concentrations, provided a very good fit with the experimental data without the need to introduce any second-order effect, such as in the sphere of action model or diffusion-limited processes. Moreover, by changing the dilution of the UFNP solution, we could study how the quenching ratio depended on the fluorophore concentration, whereas it is predicted to be constant by the standard model. In addition to providing a significant test of the nonlinear model for static quenching, we believe this study could help find the best parameters for the optimization of sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio in applications such as fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: As is well known within the chemistry and biochemistry communities, fluorescence quenching generally refers to any process that decreases the fluorescence intensity of a given compound or fluorophore through the interaction with a specific chemical or physical agent, the quencher [1]. In particular, the case of a fluorophore embedded in a relatively complex molecule or aggregate - such as a protein or a nanoparticle - interacting with a chemical quencher – such as a certain ion or molecule - is of great interest for both fundamental research and applications. On the one side, this type of fluorescence quenching involves a variety of possible mechanisms and different processes, which has stimulated a great deal of studies [2–9] and will also be the main topic of this work. On the other side, it can be a useful tool in chemistry and biomolecular research since can provide information about properties such as protein conformation [10], DNA analysis [11], reactivity and diffusion coefficients [12], etc. In addition, fluorescence quenching is also used in fluorescent chemical sensors, where the presence of small traces of a specific analyte is revealed by the decrease of the light emitted by a sensing material [13–18]. In any case, the analysis of the experimental data is first aimed at attributing the nature of the quenching effect, whether it is a dynamic quenching – e.g. due to collisional deactivation – or resulting from binding and the formation of a stable nonfluorescent complex between the fluorophore and quencher, often referred to as static quenching. The simultaneous presence of both mechanisms, as well as the occurrence of other processes is also to be considered and deviations from this simplified model are far from being rare [2–9,19,20]. The attribution is generally made through the study of certain properties including but not limited to the variation of the fluorophore absorption spectrum and the dependence of the fluorescence lifetime τ and the quenching ratio I 0/I on the quencher concentration, [Q], where I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively.

In particular, in the case of dominant static quenching due to the formation of ground-state complexes, the classical Stern-Volmer model [1] states that the lifetime is independent of [Q] and remains unchanged τ = τ 0 with the addition of quencher. In addition, the quenching ratio should increase linearly with the quencher concentration as described by the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1),
(1)
I
0
I
=
1
+
K
SV
[
Q
]
where K
SV is the Stern-Volmer constant for complex formation. According to Eq. (1), a diagram of I 0/I vs. [Q], the so-called Stern-Volmer plot, should give a straight line with a slope equal to K
SV . A similar linear behaviour is also predicted by the classical model in the case of pure collisional quenching, while a quadratic dependence with an upward curvature is expected in the presence of both mechanisms. However, positive deviation from linearity, i.e. upward curvature, was also frequently observed in systems involving only one of the two processes, either dynamic or static quenching [3–5,8]. This has led to the introduction of a variety of second-order processes, such as in the “sphere of action” model [21] or in the diffusion-limited treatment [3].

It should be reminded, however, that the classical Stern-Volmer model assumes the quencher concentration much larger than the concentration of fluorophore, or [Q] > > [F]. This implies [Q] ≅ [Q 0] at any given time, regardless of the formation of a certain number of complexes. Obviously, this may not be case in sensing applications where, in fact, low detection limits are pursued, and quite the opposite situation [Q] « [F] generally occurs. This constraint and the possible misuse of classical model could explain some recent results on fluorescence quenching in graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) in the presence of heavy-metal ions [22–24]. Those studies mainly aimed at demonstrating the possibility of effective fluorescence sensing of heavy metals in water and reported Stern-Volmer plots with positive deviations from linearity, i.e. upward curvature, while a constant lifetime, the absorption spectra and other considerations of coordination chemistry strongly supported the hypothesis of a dominant static quenching originating from the formation of stable ground-state complexes. Within this framework, a more general model for static quenching extended to any value of fluorophore/quencher concentrations was recently developed by Campbell et al. [20] and applied to the fluorescence quenching of poly(para-phenylene ethynylenes) by C60 fullerenes. The comparison with experimental data, however, was still limited to the range [Q] > [F] and yet significant variations from linearity were found.

In this study, to investigate the discrepancies reported in previous reports [22–24], we have measured the fluorescence quenching in graphene-like nanoparticles, the fluorophore, interacting with Cu2+ ions, the quencher, in a broad range of the respective concentrations. The carbon nanoparticles were obtained by cage opening and unfolding of C60 fullerene and are hereafter named unfolded-fullerene nanoparticles (UFNPs). The concentration of UFNPs was varied in the interval 7 – 250 μM while the concentration of Cu2+ ranged from 1 to 200 μM, thus including but not limited to the typical situation for sensing applications, i.e. [Q] « [F]. It should be noted that the present study extends and improves our previous experiment with the same system [24] in that a larger range of Cu2+ ion concentration and a higher resolution of the sampling values were investigated. Most importantly, the concentration of the graphene-like nanoparticles was also varied and it spanned a large and significant interval. This improved methodology allowed us to analyse the experimental results by using the model for static quenching by Campbell et al. [20].

All the reagents were used as supplied, without further purification. Sulfuric acid (99.9%), sodium nitrate, sodium hydroxide, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide and copper nitrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. C60 Buckminsterfullerene was acquired from TERM, USA. Deionized water was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore). For titration analysis, copper salt solutions were prepared at the concentration of both 1 mM and 10 mM and stored in the dark at 4 C° until use. UFNPs were synthesized from C60 fullerene by a modified Hummers method, as reported previously [24–26]. Briefly, a one-pot reaction based on a strong chemical oxidation was performed in water using NaNO3, KMnO4 and H2SO4 at a maximum temperature of 75°. The reaction was then quenched with H2O2 and the pH was adjusted to 8 with NaOH 1 M. The residual compounds were finally removed by enclosing the water dispersion in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 2 kDa) and purified by dialyzing in 3 L deionized water for 48 h to obtain the UFNP stock solution. The molar concentration of UFNPs was estimated by weighing the dye residue of the stock solution.

Fluorescence measurements were performed with a standard laboratory set up for photoluminescence (PL) acquisition. Either a 200-W continuous Hg(Xe) discharge lamp (Oriel Corp.) with an excitation 25-cm monochromator (Photon Technology International, Inc.) or a 10-mW continuous-wave LED at 300 nm (Thorlab) were used as excitation sources. An emission 25-cm monochromator (Cornerstone 260, Oriel Corp.) with specific excitation-rejection filters and a R3896 photomultiplier (Hamamatsu Photonics K.K.) were employed to analyse and reveal the photoluminescence signal for the final digital elaboration. PL measurements were carried out at the excitation wavelength of λex = 300 nm with a spectral band-pass of 3 nm, approximately, for both the excitation and emission light. Since the conventional 90° geometry for PL measurements was adopted, particular care was taken to reduce possible inner-filter effects [19]. In particular, the investigated volume of sample was restricted to the portion close to the entrance and lateral sides, even though standard squared fused-silica cuvettes with an optical length of 10 mm were used. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by using a Cary 50 spectrophotometer (Varian, Inc., USA). The reference UFNP solutions for optical measurements were prepared by diluting the stock solution with deionized water so as to achieve the required samples of UFNPs with concentration in the range of 5–200 μM. The measurements in the presence of metal ions were performed by mixing 1.5 mL of the reference solution with 1.5 mL of the metal-salt water solution at the appropriate concentration. Transmittance and PL spectra were recorded after stirring for 60 s and were compared with measurements on the blank sample prepared by mixing 1.5 mL of the reference solution with 1.5 mL of deionized water. In order to test the reproducibility of the synthesis procedure to the end of optical properties, measurements were repeated on samples coming from four different batches produced on distinct days.

A complete characterization of the morphology and properties of UFNPs, whose expected structure is schematically displayed in Fig. 1a, is out of the scope of this report and can be found in our previous works [24,25]. In particular, the stable aqueous suspension used in this study was prepared by the same method and exhibited the same chemical and physical characteristics as reported elsewhere [24–26]. The most important properties, for the purpose of the present study, are related to the absorption spectrum, which gives the UFNP solution a brownish colour with no appreciable Tyndall effect, and the UV-induced blue photoluminescence (PL) with a broad band centred at 445 nm, as displayed in Fig. 1b.

These characteristics are similar to those found in regular GOQDs prepared both from graphite, with a top-down method or from citric acid with a bottom-up synthesis [22,23,27–31]. In particular, the absorption spectrum exhibits the typical peak at 230 nm due to the π-π * transition of the sp2-carbon residual domains in the graphene-like lattice. A much smoother shoulder at ∼305 nm is generally attributed to the n-π * transition in the C˭O bonds of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as the hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy moieties that decorate the carbon structure (see Fig. 1a). The role of these functional groups also lies in providing hydrophilicity to UFNPs and stability to water dispersions, due to the negative charge acquired form the nanoparticles which prevents aggregation. In fact, the optical absorbance of stock solution did not change profile and decreased linearly with the concentration down to a dilution factor of 40, indicating the lack of concentration-induced aggregation. In addition, the chemical and optical properties of UFNP solutions have remained stable for more than 1 year.

As displayed in Fig. 2, the addition of Cu(NO3)2 progressively decreased the fluorescence intensity of the UFNP solution, without appreciable changes of the spectral profile, with a typical fluorescence quenching effect. The present data extend and improve the resolution of the values of concentration of our previous works [24,25] where we demonstrated that the quenching effect was uniquely due to the presence of Cu2+ ions, which thus represent the quencher of the present experiment, without any influence from the anionic species composing the copper salt. The constant lifetime of the quenched fluorescence and the variation of the UV–vis absorbance (not shown here, see Ref [25]) indicated the occurrence of a static quenching due to the formation of stable nonfluorescent complexes between UFNPs and Cu2+ ions. Chelation of copper ions by graphene-based nanoparticles can, in fact, occur through the deprotonated carboxyl groups dangling from the carbon structure, as demonstrated in several studies dealing with both graphene oxide microflakes and carbon nanotubes [32–34]. Charge transfer from the excited UFNPs to the electron-withdrawing copper ion would thus be the origin of the observed fluorescence quenching. [35,36].

Fig. 3 shows the Stern-Volmer plot I
0
/I of the quenching experiment carried out with two different concentrations of UFNPs. Specifically, the upper data (solid circles) refer to the solution that was used in the quenching experiment of Fig. 2 and obtained by diluting the reference solution 1:2 with deionized water. The lower data (triangles) refer the reference solution as it is. In both cases, experimental results exhibited an appreciable nonlinear behaviour with an upward curvature at low values of Cu2+ concentration. As already mentioned under “Introduction”, this behaviour had already been reported in other systems involving formation of stable complexes, also based on molecular fluorophore/quencher, which should thus exhibit a linear plot [5,8,22]. In the present case, however, the upward curvature remarkably turned to a linear growth at higher values of concentration of Cu2+.

The experimental data were fitted by using the generalized model for static quenching by Campbell et al. [20] which assumes variable populations of fluorophore and quencher at any given time according to the equations:
(2)
[ F Q ]
=
K
[ F ]
[ Q ]
[ F ]
=
[
F
0
]
−
[ F Q ]
[ Q ]
=
[
Q
0
]
−
[ F Q ]
where [F 0] ([Q 0]) is the initial fluorophore (quencher) concentration, [F] ([Q]) is the fluorophore (quencher) concentration at any time after a certain concentration of complexes [FQ] has formed through the association constant K. From the model, a nonlinear equation for the quenching ratio is obtained
(3)
I
0
I
=
1
1

1

SV
+

0
]
]

As can be seen in Fig. 3, experimental data can be accurately fitted using Eq. (3) where F 0 and Q 0 are the experimental concentrations of UNFPs and Cu2+, respectively. The fit is good for both values of fluorophore concentration and a relatively large spectrum of quencher concentrations. In particular, the generalized model successfully describes the transition of the experimental data from upward curvature to linear growth with increasing quencher concentration. To this end, it should be noted that [F 0] was considered a fit parameter of Eq. (3) due to the uncertainty of the real UNFP concentration. The best fit was found with a value of the Stern-Volmer constant
K
SV
=
1.0 ×
10
5
M
− 1
and a fluorophore concentrations of [UFNP]1 = 30 μM, for the solution exhibiting the upper data (solid circles), and [UFNP]2 = 60 μM for the second solution (triangles). The obtained value of K
SV lies in the range of those reported for the interaction of Cu2+ with regular GOQDs [22,37], it is significantly higher than the value 1.1 × 104 M−1 measured in the quenching of poly(para-phenylene ethynylenes) by C60 fullerenes [20] and is lower, as expected, than the value
K
SV
=
1.9 ×
10
7
M
− 1
found in a molecule-polymer system [8]. The concentrations of carbon nanoparticles that provided the best fit are in agreement with the values that were estimated by weighing the dry residue and considering the expected molecular weight (MW) of UFNPs, as depicted in Fig. 1a, with some additional assumptions. Specifically, we considered single cage opening of C60 fullerenes with the consequent average MW of fragments, a certain content of functional groups with a 1:2 oxygen/carbon atom ratio [38] and the occurrence of two- or three-layer nanoparticle stacking. Even though all these hypotheses are consistent with previous characterization by Dynamic Light Scattering, Atomic Force Microscopy, X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy and Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy [24,25], too many uncertainties remain to state that a good fit has been achieved also for the UFNP concentration.

However, besides the numerical results, the above considerations highlight the fact that the [F 0] can be parameter of Eq. (3) to the fit experimental data since the quenching ratio I 0/I does depend on the fluorophore concentration. This is a peculiar characteristics of the nonlinear model since Eq. (1) of the classical Stern-Volmer equation is independent of [F 0], instead. To this regard, in order to further test the agreement with the generalized model, we measured the dependence of the quenching effect on the fluorophore concentration at two fixed values of quencher concentration. Fig. 4 shows the fractional fluorescence quenching (I 0 -I)/I 0 as a function of the dilution factor of the UNFP solution for a quencher concentration of [Cu2+] = 50 μM (stars). The continuous line represents the nonlinear equation derived from the generalized model [20]
(4)
I
0
−
I
I
0
=
1
2

]

SV
+

]

]

4

0
]
[
Q
0
]
]

In particular, experimental data and the model results are reported in Fig. 4 as a function of the dilution factor for two reasons. First, this representation reflects the simplest experimental method, and the one we used, to scale down the UNFP concentration precisely starting from the stock solution. However, the values of the molar concentration, estimated by the previous fit of Fig. 3, are reported on the top axis. Secondly, it shows more clearly an interesting and somehow surprising characteristics of the generalized model. In fact, according to Eq. (4), the fractional fluorescence quenching does not tend to 1, but to a finite value depending on [Cu2+], as the fluorophore concentration [UNFP] approaches zero (or as the dilution factor tends to infinity, see Fig. 4). In the present case, with a quencher concentration of 50 µM, the fractional quenching tends to
0.8
3
̅
. Differently, inspection of Eq. (4) shows that the fractional quenching tends to 1 as the quencher concentration tends towards infinity, [Cu2+] → ∞ , as expected. Obviously, this cannot be seen in Fig. 4, where the quencher concentration is constant, and would require an additional plot. In other words, the increase of the quencher concentration towards infinity is not equivalent to decreasing the fluorophore concentration towards zero, due to the asymmetry of Eq. (4) with respect to [F 0] and [Q 0].

As regards the agreement with the experimental data, the model reasonably predicts the behaviour of the fractional quenching up to a dilution factor of 12, which corresponds to a fluorophore concentration of [UNFP] = 25 μM, approximately. For higher dilution factors, the model seems to systematically underestimate the experimental results. It should be noted, however, that the high dilution range is likely to be affected by larger experimental uncertainties due to the low intensity of the fluorescence signal. On the other side, experimental data confirmed the general trend predicted by the nonlinear model, and that is the increase of fractional quenching with decreasing concentration of fluorophore. We believe that this finding is particularly important from the point of view of applications to sensing, where the maximum signal for a certain quencher concentration is pursued. Obviously, when the fluorophore concentration decreases, the signal of the blank reference reduces as well, and this comes at the expense of the signal to noise ratio. Therefore, an appropriate trade-off between the two needs should be considered by researchers in each specific situation.

In summary, our studies on the fluorescence quenching of UFNPs in the presence of Cu2+ have shown that formation of fluorophore/quencher ground-state nonfluorescent complexes can be successfully described by a generalized Stern-Volmer model for dominant static quenching without any restraint on the fluorophore/quencher concentrations. Theoretical results show a good general agreement with the experimental data, in particular as regards the transition from the positive curvature to the linear growth of the quenching ratio. In addition, the present report demonstrated that the nonlinear model also predicts the dependence of the quenching effect on the fluorophore concentration, whereas the classical model considers the quenching effect to be independent of the fluorophore concentration. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first extended test of this peculiar characteristic of the generalized model and could help improve the characterization of complex formation processes. More importantly, the present result could be useful to optimize sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio in applications involving fluorescence sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the University of Rome Tor Vergata in the framework of “GHOST - GrapHene Oxide quantum dots in Sol-gel maTerials for environmental sensing applications” project, whithin Mission: Sustainability" program (D.R. 2817/2016), grant number (CUP): E86C18000450005.
